title: How Anti-Chinese Immigration Laws Unexpectedly Led to a Chinese Restaurant Boom in America
description:
in Food & Drink, History |   	July 14th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

This past spring, the oldôÙest conôÙtinôÙuôÙousôÙly operôÙatôÙed famôÙiôÙly-owned Ch
body:
in Food & Drink, History |   	July 14th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

This past spring, the oldôÙest conôÙtinôÙuôÙousôÙly operôÙatôÙed famôÙiôÙly-owned ChiôÙnese restauôÙrant in the UnitôÙed States served its last plate of chop suey. Pekin NooôÙdle ParôÙlor had been an instiôÙtuôÙtion in Butte, MonôÙtanaãs ChiôÙnaôÙtown since 1911, long outôÙlastôÙingô the townãs gold-rush boom, butô accordôÙing to its final, fifth-genôÙerôÙaôÙtion ownôÙer, it couldôÙnãt surôÙvive changôÙing attiôÙtudes toward dinôÙing out in the twenôÙty-twenôÙties. Whether or not COVID-influôÙenced habits or delivôÙery-app addicôÙtion are to blame, the Pekinãs cloôÙsure conôÙstiôÙtutôÙed an occaôÙsion to reflect on the hisôÙtoôÙry of AmerôÙiôÙcan ChiôÙnese food, and its rapid evoôÙluôÙtion into a disôÙtinct cuiôÙsine unto itself.
Take chop suey,ô which was adverôÙtised on the Pekinãs neon sign in letôÙterôÙing largôÙer than the name of the restauôÙrant itself. Often citôÙed as an earôÙly ãChiôÙneseã dish actuôÙalôÙly inventôÙed by ChiôÙnese immiôÙgrants in the UnitôÙed States, it may have a cerôÙtain basis in the tsap seuiô eatôÙen in GuangôÙdong province from which many of them had come.
But even there, it amountôÙed to a techôÙnique for throwôÙing togethôÙer a hodgeôÙpodge of leftôÙovers in a palatôÙable manôÙner; only with its AmerôÙiôÙcanôÙizaôÙtion did it acquire a disôÙtinct set of flaôÙvors and texôÙtures. A simôÙiôÙlar process seems to have proôÙduced GenôÙerôÙal Tsoãs chickôÙen, brocôÙcoli beef, lo mein, and all the othôÙer dishôÙes that the movies have conôÙvinced the world AmerôÙiôÙcans eat directôÙly from wire-hanôÙdled paper boxôÙes.


	

WhatôÙevôÙer HolôÙlyôÙwoodãs tenôÙdenôÙcy to exagôÙgerôÙate, the popôÙuôÙlarôÙiôÙty of domesôÙtic ChiôÙnese food is real. AccordôÙing to the BusiôÙness InsidôÙer video just above, ChiôÙnese restauôÙrants outôÙnumôÙber even McDonôÙaldãs franôÙchisôÙes in the U.S. How they reached that point owes more than a litôÙtle to immiôÙgraôÙtion, as anyôÙone would expect, but also, less obviôÙousôÙly, to restricôÙtions on immiôÙgraôÙtion. ãAnti-ChiôÙnese senôÙtiôÙment was ramôÙpant in AmerôÙiôÙca in the earôÙly 20th cenôÙtuôÙry ã and had been since the latôÙter half of the 19th cenôÙtuôÙry, when as many as 300,000 ChiôÙnese minôÙers, farmôÙers, railôÙroad and facôÙtoôÙry workôÙers came to the U.S.,ã writes NPRãs Maria Godoy. The negôÙaôÙtive reacôÙtion to that influx underôÙlay the ChiôÙnese ExcluôÙsion Act of 1882; the ImmiôÙgraôÙtion Act of 1917, with its ãAsiôÙatôÙic Barred Zoneã; andô the ImmiôÙgraôÙtion Act of 1924, which introôÙduced a nationôÙal-oriôÙgin quoôÙta sysôÙtem.


	

Despite the ostenôÙsiôÙbly severe restricôÙtion on ChiôÙnese immiôÙgraôÙtion per se, the law allowed that ãsome ChiôÙnese busiôÙness ownôÙers in the U.S. could get speôÙcial merôÙchant visas that allowed them to travôÙel to ChiôÙna, and bring back employôÙees. Only a few types of busiôÙnessôÙes qualôÙiôÙfied for this staôÙtus. In 1915, a fedôÙerôÙal court added restauôÙrants to that list. Voila! A restauôÙrant boom was born.ã DitchôÙing their traôÙdiôÙtionôÙal busiôÙnessôÙes like launôÙdries, ChiôÙnese in the U.S. would ãpool their monôÙey to start luxôÙuôÙry ãchop suey palaces,ã then each investor would take turns runôÙning the joint for a year or 18 monthsã in order to earn merôÙchant staôÙtus. What susôÙtained it all was the increasôÙingôÙly insaôÙtiable AmerôÙiôÙcan demand for the food these immiôÙgrants had perôÙfectôÙed, from chop suey to kung pao chickôÙen toô moo goo gai pan and beyond. The stoôÙry neatôÙly arrives at an AmerôÙiôÙcan-style moral: where thereãs a will, thereãs a way ã or rather, yúu zhû˜ zhá, shû˜ jû˜ng chûˋng.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
The 63 Cuisines of ChiôÙna Explained in 40 MinôÙutes: A ComôÙplete Primer
A Brief HisôÙtoôÙry of Dumplings: An AniôÙmatôÙed IntroôÙducôÙtion
The SurôÙprisôÙing ReaôÙson Why ChiôÙnaôÙtowns WorldôÙwide Share the Same AesôÙthetôÙic, and How It All StartôÙed with the 1906 San FranôÙcisôÙco EarthôÙquake
ColôÙorôÙful AniôÙmaôÙtion VisuôÙalôÙizes 200 Years of ImmiôÙgraôÙtion to the U.S. (1820-Present)
Bob Dylan PotaôÙto Chips, AnyôÙone?: What Theyãre SnackôÙing on in ChiôÙna
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Explore 1,300+ Beautiful Wildlife Illustrations from the 19th Century, Now Restored and Available Online
description:

Today, if you want to know about any of the floôÙra or fauôÙna that surôÙround us, you hav
body:
in Books, Nature |   	July 15th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Today, if you want to know about any of the floôÙra or fauôÙna that surôÙround us, you have only to look it up online. After you get your fill of knowlôÙedge, you can decide whether or not you want to venôÙture out into the world and see your object of interôÙest in its natôÙurôÙal enviôÙronôÙment (or a conôÙtrolled simôÙuôÙlaôÙtion thereôÙof). In the VicôÙtoôÙriôÙan era, things worked a bit difôÙferôÙentôÙly. IdeôÙalôÙly, youãd have grown up in a houseôÙhold, or at least had access to an instiôÙtuôÙtion, with the comôÙplete set of The NatôÙuôÙralôÙistãs Library, a series of more than 40 volôÙumes on everyôÙthing from the birds and the bees to the quadrupeds and the marôÙsuôÙpiôÙalia. PrintôÙed in a relôÙaôÙtiveôÙly small forôÙmat and priced at six shillings each, they brought the intelôÙlecôÙtuôÙal fruits of the natôÙuôÙralôÙistãs enterôÙprise closôÙer to the reach of the everyôÙman than ever before.

While these books offered a good deal of inforôÙmaôÙtive text, includôÙing memôÙoirs from varôÙiôÙous famous natôÙuôÙralôÙists of the time, their immeôÙdiôÙate attracôÙtion had more to do with their gloôÙriôÙous illusôÙtraôÙtions, in which colôÙored examôÙples of each species popped right out of its black-and-white habiôÙtat. These more than 1,300 colôÙor plates, some of the finest that could be seen in any pubôÙliôÙcaôÙtion of simôÙiôÙlar scale in the mid-nineôÙteenth cenôÙtuôÙry, preôÙsentôÙed an attracôÙtive project to the designôÙer Nicholas Rougeux, whose work weãve preôÙviôÙousôÙly feaôÙtured here on Open CulôÙture.
HavôÙing already restored and creôÙatôÙed digôÙiôÙtal verôÙsions of EuclidôÙãsô EleôÙments, Pierre-Joseph RedôÙoutûˋãs Les RosôÙes and Les LilôÙiôÙacûˋes, ElizôÙaôÙbeth TwinôÙingãs IllusôÙtraôÙtions of the NatôÙurôÙal Orders of Plants, andô Daniel BerkeôÙley Updikeãsô PrintôÙing Types, among othôÙer books, heãs now put online aô comôÙplete reproôÙducôÙtion of the NatôÙuôÙralôÙistãs Libraryô ã with, as usuôÙal, a blog post about the painstakôÙing restoraôÙtion and digôÙiôÙtal re-creôÙation process.

This time, Rougeux has includôÙed a secôÙtion about his use of artiôÙfiôÙcial intelôÙliôÙgence, which actuôÙalôÙly did its part to bring The NatôÙuôÙralôÙistãs Library to his attenôÙtion in the first place. Not only did AI tools then help him unearth needôÙed sources and fill in visuôÙal gaps, they also came in handy when he was brainôÙstormôÙing covôÙer conôÙcepts for a printôÙed verôÙsion. Though Rougeuxãs restoraôÙtion is priôÙmarôÙiôÙly a web site, free to all to explore, you can also buy your own handôÙsome, large-forôÙmat physôÙiôÙcal copy of Plates of the NatôÙuôÙralôÙistãs Library for $295.11 USD.

EasôÙiôÙer on the walôÙlet is the series of posters heãs made with the same illusôÙtraôÙtions, each of which presents one of these catôÙeôÙgories of creaôÙtures great or small at a glance. The origôÙiôÙnal NatôÙuôÙralôÙistãs Library inspired genôÙerôÙaôÙtions to dedôÙiôÙcate themôÙselves to underôÙstandôÙing the natôÙurôÙal world; these new verôÙsions, whether in print, online, or on the wall, will no doubt encourôÙage the enthuôÙsiôÙasm of more than a few budôÙding natôÙuôÙralôÙists in the genôÙerôÙaôÙtions to come. VisôÙit theô reproôÙducôÙtion of the NatôÙuôÙralôÙistãs Library here.

Behold an InterôÙacôÙtive Online EdiôÙtion of ElizôÙaôÙbeth Twiningãs IllusôÙtraôÙtions of the NatôÙurôÙal Orders of Plants (1868)
Ernst Haeckelãs SubôÙlime DrawôÙings of FloôÙra & FauôÙna: The BeauôÙtiôÙful SciôÙenôÙtifôÙic DrawôÙings That InfluôÙenced Europeãs Art NouôÙveau MoveôÙment (1889)

Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: 11,700 Free Photos from John Margoliesã Archive of Americana Architecture: Download, Use & Re-Mix
description: Many conôÙnoisôÙseurs of archiôÙtecôÙture are enthralled by the modôÙernist phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy of Le CorôÙbusier, Frank Lloyd Wright, and I M Pei, who share
body:
Many conôÙnoisôÙseurs of archiôÙtecôÙture are enthralled by the modôÙernist phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy of Le CorôÙbusier, Frank Lloyd Wright, and I M Pei, who shared a belief that form folôÙlows funcôÙtion, or, as Wright had it, that form and funcôÙtion are one.
OthôÙers of us delight in gas staôÙtions shaped like teapots and restauôÙrants shaped like fish or doughôÙnuts. If thereãs a phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy behind these insisôÙtentôÙly playôÙful visions, it likeôÙly has someôÙthing to do with joyãÎand pulling in tourists.

Art hisôÙtoôÙriôÙan John MarôÙgoliesô (1940ã2016), respondôÙing to the beauôÙty of such quirky visions, scramôÙbled to preôÙserve the eviôÙdence, transôÙformôÙing into a respectôÙed, self-taught phoôÙtogôÙraôÙphôÙer in the process. A GuggenôÙheim FounôÙdaôÙtion grant and the finanôÙcial supôÙport of archiôÙtect Philip JohnôÙson allowed him to log over four decadesã worth of trips on Americaãs blue highôÙways, hopôÙing to capôÙture his quarôÙry before it disôÙapôÙpeared for good.
Despite Johnsonãs patronôÙage, and his own stints as an ArchiôÙtecôÙturôÙal Record ediôÙtor and ArchiôÙtecôÙturôÙal League of New York proôÙgram direcôÙtor, he seemed to welôÙcome the rufôÙfled minôÙiôÙmalôÙist feathôÙers his enthuôÙsiôÙasm for mini golf coursôÙes, theme motels, and eye-catchôÙing roadôÙside attracôÙtions occaôÙsioned.
On the othôÙer hand, he resentôÙed when his pasôÙsions were labelled as ãkitsch,ã a point that came across in a 1987 interôÙview with the CanaôÙdiôÙan Globe and Mail:
PeoôÙple genôÙerôÙalôÙly have thought that whatãs imporôÙtant are the large, unique archiôÙtecôÙturôÙal monôÙuôÙments. They think Torontoãs City Hall is imporôÙtant, but not those wonôÙderôÙful gnomeãsãcastle gas staôÙtions in ToronôÙto, a Detroit influôÙence that crept across the borôÙder and polôÙlutôÙed your wonôÙderôÙfulôÙly conôÙserôÙvôÙaôÙtive enviôÙronôÙment.

As MarôÙgolies foreôÙsaw, the type of comôÙmerôÙcial verôÙnacôÙuôÙlar archiôÙtecôÙture heãd loved since boyhoodãthe type that screams, ãLook at me! Look at meããhas become very nearôÙly extinct.
And that is a maxôÙiôÙmal shame.
Your chilôÙdren may not be able to visôÙit an orange juice stand shaped like an orange or the LeanôÙing TowôÙer of PizôÙza, but thanks to the Library of ConôÙgress, these locales can be pitôÙstops on any virôÙtuôÙal famôÙiôÙly vacaôÙtion you might underôÙtake.

In July 2017, the library selectôÙed the John MarôÙgolies RoadôÙside AmerôÙiôÙca PhoôÙtoôÙgraph Archive as its ãfree to use and reuseã colôÙlecôÙtion. So linger as long as youãd like and do with these 11,700+ images as you willãmake postôÙcards, tãshirts, souôÙvenir placeôÙmats.
(Or eschew your comôÙputôÙer entirelyãgo on a real road trip, and conôÙtinôÙue MarôÙgoliesã work!)
WhatôÙevôÙer you decide to do with them, the archiveãs homeôÙpage has tips for how to best search the 11,710 colôÙor slides conôÙtained thereôÙin. Library staffers have supôÙpleôÙmentôÙed MarôÙgoliesã notes on each image with subôÙject and geoôÙgraphôÙiôÙcal headôÙings.

Begin your jourôÙney through the Library of ConôÙgressã John MarôÙgolies RoadôÙside AmerôÙiôÙca PhoôÙtoôÙgraph Archive here.
Weãd love to see your vacaôÙtion snaps upon your return.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2017.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
Watch StewôÙart Brandãs 6ãPart Series How BuildôÙings Learn, With Music by BriôÙan Eno
Frank Lloyd Wright Designs an Urban Utopia: See His Hand-Drawn SketchôÙes of BroadôÙacre City (1932)
A is for ArchiôÙtecôÙture: 1960 DocôÙuôÙmenôÙtary on Why We Build, from the Ancient Greeks to ModôÙern Timesô 
Watch 50+ DocôÙuôÙmenôÙtaries on Famous ArchiôÙtects & BuildôÙings: Bauhaus, Le CorôÙbusier, Hadid & Many More
Ayun HalôÙlôÙiôÙday is an author, illusôÙtraôÙtor, and theôÙater makôÙer in NYC.

title: How Russian Artists Imagined in 1914 What Moscow Would Look Like in 2259
description:
in History, Sci Fi |   	July 14th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
In the days of popôÙuôÙlar retroôÙfuôÙturôÙismãsay, the first half of the twenôÙtiôÙeth cent
body:
in History, Sci Fi |   	July 14th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
In the days of popôÙuôÙlar retroôÙfuôÙturôÙismãsay, the first half of the twenôÙtiôÙeth centuryãpeople tendôÙed to imagôÙine the world of tomorôÙrow lookôÙing very much like the world of today, only with a lot more flyôÙing cars, monoôÙrails, and videoôÙphones. This is true whether those doing the imagôÙinôÙing were titans of indusôÙtry, marôÙketôÙing mavens, ideôÙalôÙisôÙtic SoviôÙets, or subôÙjects of the Tsar, though we might think that peoôÙple livôÙing under an ancient monarôÙchiôÙcal sysôÙtem might not expect much change. In some ways we might be right, but as we can see in the 1914 postôÙcards hereãprinted as RusôÙsia entered World War Iãthe counôÙtry did anticôÙiôÙpate a modôÙern, techôÙnoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal future, though one that still closeôÙly resemôÙbled its present.

PerôÙhaps few but the most far-sightôÙed of RusôÙsians preôÙdictôÙed what the ailôÙing empire would endure in the years to comeãthe disôÙasôÙter of the Great War, and the waves of RevôÙoôÙluôÙtion and CivôÙil War. CerôÙtainôÙly, whoôÙevôÙer paintôÙed these images foreôÙsaw no such catôÙaôÙstrophôÙic upheaval.
Although purôÙportôÙing to show us a view of Moscow in the 23rd cenôÙtuôÙry, they show the city very hapôÙpiôÙly ãstill under monarôÙchiôÙcal rule,ã writes A JourôÙney Through RussôÙian CulôÙture, going about its daiôÙly life just as it did over three hunôÙdred years earôÙliôÙer, ãwith the addiôÙtion of everyôÙthing from subôÙways to airôÙborne pubôÙlic transôÙportaôÙtion, things probôÙaôÙbly seen as stanôÙdard methôÙods of transôÙport for the future.ã

Of course, there would be hot-rodôÙded sleds on St. PetersôÙburg HighôÙway with headôÙlights, fanôÙcy windôÙshields, and what look like ChristôÙmas elves perched in them. LubyanôÙsôÙka Square, furôÙther up, would still host milôÙiôÙtary parades of men on horseôÙback, as chilôÙdren whiz by on motorôÙbikes and subôÙway trains rumôÙble underôÙneath. The CenôÙtral RailôÙway StaôÙtion, above, might seem entireôÙly unchanged, until one looks up, and sees eleôÙvatôÙed trams streamôÙing out of the terôÙmiôÙnal like spiderãs silk. Red Square, howôÙevôÙer, just below, would apparôÙentôÙly host drag races, while peoôÙple in trams and giant diriôÙgiôÙbles look on from above.

The images have a childrenãs book qualôÙiôÙty about them and the fesôÙtive air of holôÙiôÙday cards. They were apparôÙentôÙly redisôÙcovôÙered only recentôÙly when a chocoôÙlate comôÙpaôÙny called Eyinem reprintôÙed them on their packôÙagôÙing. Like so much retroôÙfuôÙturôÙism, these seemãin their bustling, yet safe, cheerôÙful orderlinessãtailor-made for nosôÙtalôÙgic trips through PetroôÙvsky Park, rather than imagôÙiôÙnaôÙtive leaps into the great unknown. For that, we must turn to RussôÙian FuturôÙism, which, both before and after World War I and the RevôÙoôÙluôÙtion, imagôÙined, helped bring about, but didôÙnãt quite surôÙvive the masôÙsive techôÙnoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal and politôÙiôÙcal disôÙrupôÙtion of the next two decades.

Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2017.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
SoviôÙet Artists EnviôÙsion a ComôÙmuôÙnist Utopia in OutôÙer Space
How the SoviôÙets ImagôÙined in 1960 What the World Would Look in 2017: A Gallery of Retro-FuturôÙisôÙtic DrawôÙings
DownôÙload RussôÙian FuturôÙist Book Art (1910ã1915): The AesôÙthetôÙic RevôÙoôÙluôÙtion Before the PolitôÙiôÙcal RevôÙoôÙluôÙtion
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.

title: Korea's Coastal Folklore Surfaces in Jeongmin Lee's Ink Illustrations
description: Jeongmin Lee is interested in the ways ãmemory is carried through craft and repetition.ã On traditional Korean mulberry paper, or hanji, Lee draws
body:
Jeongmin Lee is interested in the ways ãmemory is carried through craft and repetition.ã On traditional Korean mulberry paper, or hanji, Lee draws delicate lines in ink and pigments known as bunchae, rendering rippling textures that whirl across the page. Steeped in local folklore and mythology, the Busan-based artist creates surreal scenes that conjure fantastical tales of life by the sea.
ãMost of my recent projects begin with reading regional folktales, visiting places connected to those stories, and collecting fragments of history, mythology, and oral traditions,ã she says. ãI rarely paint a folktale exactly as itãs written; Iãm more interested in its symbols, emotions, and the questions it leaves behind.ã

Many of Leeãs compositions focus on womenãs knowledge, labor, and resilience and how those qualities emerge through coastal storytelling. Tales of powerful sea gods and the diving traditions of the haenyeo commingle into balanced illustrations that translate the symbols and motifs of the region anew.
While her approach is labor-intensive, Lee enjoys utilizing such a meticulous, meditative technique. ãPainting with traditional pigments requires a slow, layered process. The colors are built gradually, allowing the paper and pigments to create subtle textures that wouldnãt be possible with other materials,ã she adds. ãIt gives me space to sit with these stories while I paint them.
Many of the works shown here are part of Daughters of the Sea(s), an ongoing series on view alongside artist Kato Trofimova for the SáBRS Festival in Riga. Lee hopes to expand the project into one thatãs participatory and connects similar narratives from across the globe. ãIãm exploring ways that folklore can become something people experience and talk about together, rather than something that exists only on a gallery wall,ã Lee shares.
Sheãs also currently working on a graphic novel centering on Busanãs mythology and folklore, which she hopes to complete next year. Keep up with her projects on Instagram.

schema.headline: Korea's Coastal Folklore Surfaces in Jeongmin Lee's Ink Illustrations

title: Divine Sculptures Revere a Yorû¿bûÀ Goddess in Nigeria's Ã£Ã¿Èun-ûÃ¿Èogbo Sacred Grove
description: In Yorû¿bûÀ culture, itãs said that more than 600 years ago, a hunter discovered a lush grove in southwestern Nigeria carved by a rushing river. His
body:
In Yorû¿bûÀ culture, itãs said that more than 600 years ago, a hunter discovered a lush grove in southwestern Nigeria carved by a rushing river. His community had experienced drought and eagerly moved to the region, which they quickly learned was under the rule of the goddess of rivers and fertility, Ã£äÃ¿Èun. In exchange for protection and prosperity, the people promised to celebrate the deity, and this pact grounds whatãs now known as the Ã£Ã¿Èun-ûÃ¿Èogbo Sacred Grove. 
A UNESCO World Heritage site spanning 190 acres, the spiritual sanctuary has long been revered by the Yorû¿bûÀ people, and in the mid-20th century, a group of artists revitalized the landscape by erecting large-scale sculptures in honor of its namesake. Dubbed the New Sacred Art Movement, the efforts are the subject of a short documentary released by The Met that visits the grove and highlights some of the artists whoãve carved totems, shaped enormous creatures from clay and mud, and established a vibrant art environment to be passed down through generations.



Directed by Sosena Solomon, the film is part of The Metãs series devoted to Africaãs cultural landmarks, made in collaboration with the World Monuments Fund. It highlights some of the makers creating and repairing works, including Kasali Akangbe Ogun, who helped lead the New Sacred Art Movement alongside Austrian-Nigerian artist Susanne Wenger and Chief Adebisi Akanji in the 1960s. Today, he continues to carve totemic shrines and share his craft with his children.
While many similar sites fell into disrepair, the artists who worked in the grove helped to safeguard its sacredness as they built an expansive art environment visualizing various deities. An annual festival and pilgrimages attract visitors each year, and as generations pass, artists and caretakers are working to both preserve whatãs been built and also pass down knowledge to ensure the spaceãs survival. ãWhat makes Ã£Ã¿Èun-ûÃ¿Èogbo such a special place is,ã says Chief Nike Davies-Okundaye, ãthis is a living story.ã
Ã£Ã¿Èun-ûÃ¿Èogbo Sacred Grove is one of 13 cultural sites The Met visited across the continent, and you can find more on YouTube. You might also enjoy this book surveying more than 400 spiritual environments around the world.

schema.headline: Divine Sculptures Revere a Yorû¿bûÀ Goddess in Nigeria's Ã£Ã¿Èun-ûÃ¿Èogbo Sacred Grove

title: Uncanny Landscapes in Pen and Ink Span Wooden Panels by John Buck
description: From the Three Patriarchs of Zion Canyon to the swamps of Louisiana to the immense cascade of Niagara Falls, John Buckãs landscapes evoke the juxt
body:
From the Three Patriarchs of Zion Canyon to the swamps of Louisiana to the immense cascade of Niagara Falls, John Buckãs landscapes evoke the juxtapositions and proportions of dreams. His solo exhibition, Mont Blanc on Wood at Zolla / Lieberman Gallery, draws us to the fuzzy boundary between the familiar and the uncanny.
The Bozeman-based artist is known for his eccentric, often life-size wooden sculptures that draw on folklore, personal memory, and daily observations. Figures are sometimes hybridized with other objects, and idiosyncratic drawings on wood panel reveal expansive landscapes populated by anthropomorphized plants and dramatic rock pinnacles.
ãLighthouseã (2024), pen and ink on wood, 27 x 36 ô§ inches
The works in Mont Blanc on Wood span a range of places and references, emphasizing landscapes where things feel perhaps a little ãoff.ã The desert stares back in ãCactus Grove (No. 13),ã and in ãLighthouse (No. 18),ã a light station in the middle of a desert lake shines mostly onto arid, rocky landforms.
ãBuck reflects on social and political realities, environmental concerns, and the eccentricities of human behavior, all while maintaining a sense of humor and a deep engagement with craft,ã the gallery says. ãHis art balances storytelling with formal clarity, inviting viewers into a world where the familiar becomes speculative and symbolic.ã
Mont Blanc on Wood continues through August 8 in Chicago.
